reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: No mandatory style violations: no hand-authored YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, correct CodeBlock intellisense usage for all Stacktape config examples.
Opening paragraph is exactly the right length (self-contained, ~70 words) and reads well as an AI-extractable snippet.
Resource categories table is an excellent orientation device for new readers — covers all resource types with AWS underpinnings, no invented entries.

The 'Connecting resources' section grounded the concrete env var names (STP_ORDERS_DB_CONNECTION_STRING, STP_ORDERS_DB_HOST, STP_ORDERS_DB_PORT) against the resource name used in the example, matching the source JSDoc.
Warning callout on resource renaming is the most important gotcha for new users working with stateful resources — well-placed and correctly framed.
FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS-behavior, and comparison/choosing questions. 9 entries is appropriate depth for a concept page of this scope.
The $ResourceParam snippet in a plain fenced TypeScript block is a fragment (no defineConfig, no class instantiation) — correctly doesn't trigger the CodeBlock requirement.
'The stack as a unit' section gives the right mental model for beginners without over-explaining CloudFormation.
DeploymentConfig properties (terminationProtection, disableAutoRollback) are correctly grounded in the source interface.
problems: 'Resource naming rules' section claims 'Alphanumeric characters and hyphens only. Must start with a letter.' — these constraints are not present in the provided source file. Presenting them as Stacktape-specific constraints without source backing is a factual accuracy risk.

The cross-stack FAQ answer references '$CfStackOutput' directive by name, but this directive does not appear anywhere in the provided source file. Mentioning it as a concrete directive path without source grounding could mislead readers.

The connectTo example says 'the processOrder function receives STP_ORDERS_DB_CONNECTION_STRING, STP_ORDERS_DB_HOST, and STP_ORDERS_DB_PORT as environment variables automatically.' The ResourceAccessProps JSDoc in the source also shows JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING for RelationalDatabase but it is omitted. While the list is not claimed to be exhaustive, the phrasing 'receives [exactly these 3]' could read as complete. Low severity, but worth softening.

The 'How resources map to AWS' deploy sequence is described as 5 numbered steps, but steps 3 and 5 describe AWS-side behaviors (IAM/SG generation, CloudFormation provisioning). The source file is silent on whether step 3 is Stacktape-side or CloudFormation-side; framing may misattribute AWS CloudFormation behavior as Stacktape behavior.

The 'Resource naming rules' bullet 'Used as CloudFormation logical ID prefixes — changing a resource name replaces the resource' is plausible CloudFormation behavior, but not verified in the provided source. Present as AWS fact with appropriate framing rather than as a Stacktape-specific rule.
optionalImprovements: Soften the 'Resource naming rules' section to 'Resource names become CloudFormation logical ID prefixes; AWS CloudFormation requires logical IDs to use alphanumeric characters and hyphens only, starting with a letter.' This correctly attributes the constraint to AWS/CloudFormation rather than presenting it as a Stacktape-specific rule unsupported by the source file.

Remove or qualify the '$CfStackOutput' directive reference in the FAQ until it appears in the source. Replace with 'you can reference exported outputs from another stack using the cross-stack directive described in the Directives documentation.'

In the connectTo example, soften 'the processOrder function receives STP_ORDERS_DB_CONNECTION_STRING, STP_ORDERS_DB_HOST, and STP_ORDERS_DB_PORT' to 'receives connection details including STP_ORDERS_DB_CONNECTION_STRING, STP_ORDERS_DB_HOST, and STP_ORDERS_DB_PORT (among others)' to avoid implying the list is exhaustive.

The first CodeBlock example declares connectTo with variable references before those variables are assigned (connectTo: [mainDatabase, uploads] appears before mainDatabase and uploads are defined). While JavaScript hoisting handles const declarations oddly here, TypeScript strict mode would flag this. Reorder so variables are defined before referenced, or note the forward reference explicitly.

Add a one-sentence pointer in the 'Connecting resources' section to the fact that KinesisStream, SnsTopic, and other resource types beyond the two shown also participate in connectTo — the current example only shows RelationalDatabase which may give readers the false impression connectTo is limited to databases and buckets.

The FAQ answer for 'What is the difference between resources and cloudformationResources?' references a 'cloudformationResources section' — consider linking to the raw CloudFormation resources page already mentioned in the resource categories table to give the reader an immediate path forward.

Consider adding a short callout or inline note in the 'Defining resources' section that the first CodeBlock example uses a forward reference (connectTo: [mainDatabase, uploads]) which is valid in defineConfig's closure but might confuse readers new to TypeScript who expect sequential declaration.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The resource categories table is excellent — comprehensive, linked, and maps Stacktape resources to concrete AWS services. A developer can immediately locate the right resource type without reading prose.
The 'Why resources exist' section explains the abstraction with a concrete example (ECS Fargate service + ALB + IAM roles + log groups from one block) without marketing language.
The Warning callout about renaming stateful resources (CloudFormation interprets rename as delete+create) is a production gotcha that many developers hit and is often missing from infra docs.

The FAQ section is strong: 9 distinct questions spanning Stacktape-specific ('How do I protect a database?'), AWS-specific ('Do resources cost money when idle?'), and comparison ('One big stack vs many small ones?').
The 'How resources map to AWS' deploy flow is concrete and references real CLI commands (`compile-template`, `info:stack`) that a reader can immediately run to verify the output.
problems: CONCRETE BUG — First code example has a JavaScript temporal dead zone error: `connectTo: [mainDatabase, uploads]` is evaluated before `mainDatabase` and `uploads` are declared (they are `const` variables declared later in the same scope). Any reader copying this example will get `ReferenceError: Cannot access 'mainDatabase' before initialization` at runtime.

TYPE MISMATCH — Both code examples pass class instances to `connectTo` (e.g., `connectTo: [mainDatabase, uploads]`, `connectTo: [ordersDb]`), but the authoritative source type `ResourceAccessProps.connectTo?: string[]` defines it as a string array. The correct form is `connectTo: ['mainDatabase', 'uploads']` or `connectTo: ['ordersDb']`. One of these two facts is wrong; the code examples need to match the actual API surface.

WRONG CLI COMMAND — The FAQ answer 'What happens if a deployment fails?' says to 'run `stacktape rollback`' and links to `/cli/rollback`. The git status shows `docs/docs/cli/cf-rollback.mdx` as a tracked file. The CLI command is almost certainly `stacktape cf:rollback`, not `stacktape rollback`. This sends readers to a 404 or the wrong page.

UNSUPPORTED CLAIM — The 'Resource naming rules' section states 'Alphanumeric characters and hyphens only. Must start with a letter.' This constraint does not appear in any of the provided source files. The code examples themselves use camelCase names (`mainDatabase`, `ordersDb`) which are alphanumeric with mixed case, but no source explicitly documents the allowed character set or a leading-letter rule.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the forward reference bug in the first code example: either reorder the declarations so `mainDatabase` and `uploads` are defined before `api`, or switch to the string-based form `connectTo: ['mainDatabase', 'uploads']`. As written the code throws a ReferenceError at runtime and cannot be safely copied.

Resolve the connectTo type mismatch: if the class-based API accepts object references (not strings), add a one-sentence note in the 'Connecting resources' section confirming this. If it only accepts strings (as the source type `connectTo?: string[]` states), update both code examples to use `connectTo: ['ordersDb']` etc. and correct any surrounding prose that implies object references work.

Correct the CLI command in the FAQ rollback answer: replace `stacktape rollback` with `stacktape cf:rollback` (consistent with `cf-rollback.mdx` in the docs tree) and update the `/cli/rollback` link to `/cli/cf-rollback`.
optionalImprovements: Remove or source-ground the 'Alphanumeric characters and hyphens only. Must start with a letter.' naming rule. If you keep it, scope it explicitly to the CloudFormation logical ID suffix rather than the TypeScript config key, and add a note that the TypeScript key follows normal JS identifier rules (camelCase recommended).

The `Convex` resource type appears in `StacktapeResourceDefinition` in the source but is absent from the categories table. Either add it (perhaps under 'Platforms') or add a parenthetical noting the table covers stable GA resources only.
The `$CfStackOutput` directive is mentioned in the FAQ without a link to directive documentation. Link it to `/configuration/directives` on first mention so readers can verify the exact syntax.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Answer-first paragraph is exactly 54 words, self-contained, and citable verbatim — correctly answers 'what is this and why does it matter'.
Resource categories table is comprehensive and citation-friendly; all union members from StacktapeResourceDefinition are represented, satisfying union-type coverage discipline.

The connectTo section names the three automatic benefits (IAM permissions, security group rules, env var injection) and lists concrete variable names keyed to the example resource name (STP_ORDERS_DB_*), which is correct per the source JSDoc in ResourceAccessProps.
Warning callout about destructive resource renaming is specific, actionable, and placed exactly where a reader needs it.

FAQ mixes all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (cross-stack references, database protection), AWS service use-case (idle costs by resource type), and comparison/choosing (one big stack vs many small ones, resources vs cloudformationResources).
Heading hierarchy is clean — all H2s are short noun-form labels, FAQ uses question-form H3s as recommended.
problems: CRITICAL — First code example (Defining resources) passes TypeScript variable references to connectTo: [mainDatabase, uploads], but mainDatabase and uploads are declared with const AFTER api. In JavaScript/TypeScript, const declarations are in the temporal dead zone before their line — the LambdaFunction constructor receives [undefined, undefined] at runtime. This is a copy-paste bug that breaks the example.

Both connectTo code examples pass resource object references (mainDatabase, uploads, ordersDb) where the source type definition clearly shows connectTo?: string[] and the source JSDoc example explicitly demonstrates string literals: connectTo: ["myDatabase", "myBucket"]. If the npm package's exported types also use string[], both examples will fail TypeScript type-checking.
FAQ answer for 'What happens if a deployment fails?' links to /cli/rollback but git status shows the CLI doc is cf-rollback.mdx — the correct route is likely /cli/cf-rollback, not /cli/rollback.

Resource naming rules section asserts 'Alphanumeric characters and hyphens only. Must start with a letter.' These constraints are not grounded in the provided source files; the types show resource names as plain object keys with no explicit validation documented.

The 'Why resources exist' section opens with 'AWS has hundreds of services...' rather than naming Stacktape resources upfront. An AI extractor pulling that paragraph gets AWS context, not a self-contained fact about Stacktape resources, reducing its snippet quality.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the temporal dead zone bug in the first code example: declare mainDatabase and uploads before api, so connectTo is evaluated after those variables are assigned. Also change connectTo to use string names (connectTo: ['mainDatabase', 'uploads']) to match the source type string[] and the source JSDoc example.

Fix the second connectTo code example to use string names (connectTo: ['ordersDb']) consistent with connectTo?: string[] in ResourceAccessProps and the source JSDoc, which explicitly shows connectTo: ["myDatabase", "myBucket"].
Fix the CLI rollback link in the FAQ: update /cli/rollback to /cli/cf-rollback (or verify the correct route) — linking to a non-existent page breaks the FAQ's practical value.
optionalImprovements: Rewrite the 'Why resources exist' section opener to be explicitly self-contained: start with 'Stacktape resources are opinionated abstractions that...' rather than 'AWS has hundreds of services...' to improve AEO extractability of that paragraph.

Add JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING to the inline connectTo env var list for RelationalDatabase in the 'Connecting resources' narrative — the source ScriptEnvProps JSDoc includes it alongside CONNECTION_STRING, and omitting it will surprise users relying on JDBC drivers.

Source-ground or qualify the resource naming constraints — either cite the CloudFormation logical ID rules explicitly ('CloudFormation logical IDs must be alphanumeric; hyphens are not valid') or soften to 'use alphanumeric names' to avoid stating an incorrect constraint about hyphens.

The $ResourceParam snippet inside the 'Resource references with directives' section shows an environment property with a name/value pair but gives no prose about what params are available for a KinesisStream, StateMachine, or other resources not covered by connectTo — a one-sentence pointer to /configuration/referenceable-parameters would sharpen this section.

Consider adding a brief inline mention that UpstashRedis and PrivateService connectTo injections are documented (they appear in ResourceAccessProps JSDoc but are absent from the connectTo section's prose narrative), so the section's coverage matches the source.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured, accurately represents the `connectTo` mechanism, correctly uses the class-based `defineConfig` API, and properly links to valid navigation routes. Most claims about deployment behavior (`disableAutoRollback`, `terminationProtection`, tagging) are directly supported by the source. The main issues are: an unsupported claim about resource naming constraints (specifically hyphens), and the conceptual table treating CDN and custom domains as "resources" when they aren't standalone entries in `StacktapeResourceDefinition`.

issues.statement: Resource names must follow these constraints: Alphanumeric characters and hyphens only. Must start with a letter.
issues.reason: The provided source file (`__helpers.d.ts`) does not define resource naming constraints. These rules are not grounded in the provided source. Additionally, the draft states resource names are 'Used as CloudFormation logical ID prefixes,' but CloudFormation logical IDs are strictly alphanumeric (A-Za-z0-9) — hyphens are not permitted in CF logical IDs. If Stacktape transforms names, the source doesn't show it. The claim about hyphens could lead readers to use names that cause deployment failures.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the specific naming rules (since they aren't in the provided source) or verify from runtime validators what characters are actually allowed. If hyphens are stripped/transformed before becoming CF logical IDs, note that. If only alphanumeric + camelCase is supported, state that instead.

issues.statement: The Networking row in the resource categories table lists CDN and custom domains as resources alongside HttpApiGateway, ApplicationLoadBalancer, and NetworkLoadBalancer.
issues.reason: CDN and custom domains are not standalone entries in `StacktapeResourceDefinition`. CDN is an attachment feature for compatible resources (bucket, ALB, HTTP API Gateway, Lambda function via `StpCdnCompatibleResource`), and custom domains are a `DomainConfiguration` interface used within resources. Presenting them as peer 'resources' in the same table could mislead readers into expecting standalone resource classes. However, they do have dedicated documentation pages, so this is a framing issue rather than a hard factual error.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider adding a footnote or qualifying text for CDN and custom domains indicating they are features attached to other resources rather than standalone resource types. Alternatively, split the table into 'Resource types' and 'Features/Configuration' sections.

verifierResults.summary: The draft contains several Stacktape-specific claims that are either contradicted by the provided type source or are too broad for the available source. The most important blockers are invalid `connectTo` examples, an incorrect CLI command, an unsupported top-level claim about automatic networking, and unsupported claims about generated AWS resources and stack behavior.

issues.statement: Both Stacktape config examples pass resource instances to `connectTo`: `connectTo: [mainDatabase, uploads]` and `connectTo: [ordersDb]`.
issues.reason: The provided Stacktape config types define `connectTo` as `string[]`, with examples using resource names such as `connectTo: ["myDatabase", "myBucket"]`. Passing resource instances is not supported by this source and would mislead readers copying the examples.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the examples to pass resource names, and declare referenced resources before the workload if needed. For example: `connectTo: ['mainDatabase', 'uploads']` and `connectTo: ['ordersDb']`.

evidence.quote: connectTo?: string[];
Example: `connectTo: ["myDatabase", "myBucket"]` gives this workload full access to both resources and injects `STP_MY_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`, `STP_MY_BUCKET_NAME`, etc.

issues.statement: “You can find the logical names of child resources with `stacktape info:stack --detailed`.”
issues.reason: The authoritative JSDoc for `ResourceOverrides` says to find logical IDs with `stacktape stack-info --detailed`, not `stacktape info:stack --detailed`. The draft also uses this unsupported flag again in the FAQ.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the command wording from the source, or avoid the specific flag if the current CLI source is not available for this page. If the docs standard requires the newer CLI command, verify it against CLI source before publishing.

evidence.quote: Find resource logical IDs with `stacktape stack-info --detailed`.

issues.statement: “Stacktape translates each [resource] into the correct set of AWS CloudFormation resources, wired together with the right permissions and networking.”
issues.reason: The provided source supports CloudFormation-related concepts and `connectTo` behavior, but it does not support a page-level claim that every resource is wired with networking. `connectTo` networking is specifically scoped to databases and Redis via security group rules.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the statement: Stacktape resources are configuration objects for managed infrastructure, and `connectTo` can add IAM permissions, environment variables, and network access for supported VPC-protected resources such as relational databases and Redis.

evidence.quote: - **Opens network access** (security group rules for databases, Redis)

issues.statement: “A `WebService` resource, for example, creates an ECS Fargate service, a load balancer target group, security groups, IAM roles, CloudWatch log groups, and auto-scaling policies — all from one config block.”
issues.reason: The source says container workloads have two compute engines: Fargate by default and EC2. Presenting a WebService as creating an ECS Fargate service unconditionally is too broad. The source also does not support the exact generated AWS child-resource list for WebService on this page.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that a web service runs a container workload using Fargate by default, with EC2 available through the container resources configuration, and avoid enumerating generated child resources unless the WebService source file is included.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
